questions.questionText: The resting membrane potential of a typical neuron is approximately:
questions.explanation: The resting membrane potential (RMP) of most neurons is approximately -70 mV. It is primarily maintained by K+ leak channels and Na+/K+ ATPase pump (3 Na+ out, 2 K+ in). Skeletal muscle RMP is -90 mV. Reference: Guyton — Membrane Potentials.

questions.questionText: The Na+/K+ ATPase pump transports:
questions.optionD: Equal numbers of Na+ and K+
questions.explanation: Na+/K+ ATPase is an electrogenic pump: 3 Na+ OUT, 2 K+ IN per ATP. Ouabain/digitalis inhibit it. Consumes ~30-40% of cell's ATP. Creates the ionic gradients essential for action potentials. Reference: Guyton — Active Transport.

questions.questionText: Primary active transport differs from secondary active transport in that primary active transport:
questions.optionB: Uses an electrochemical gradient of another ion
questions.explanation: Primary active transport uses ATP directly (Na+/K+ ATPase, Ca2+ ATPase, H+/K+ ATPase). Secondary uses the electrochemical gradient created by primary transport (SGLT-1 uses Na+ gradient to co-transport glucose). Reference: Guyton — Membrane Transport.

questions.questionText: The total body water in a 70 kg adult male is approximately:
questions.explanation: TBW ≈ 60% of body weight in adult males = 42L. Distribution: ICF ≈ 2/3 (28L), ECF ≈ 1/3 (14L). ECF: plasma (3L) + interstitial fluid (11L). Females ~50%, neonates ~75%. Measured by deuterium oxide dilution. Reference: Guyton — Body Fluid Compartments.

questions.questionText: The Gibbs-Donnan equilibrium describes ionic distribution across a membrane impermeable to:
questions.explanation: When non-diffusible charged macromolecules (plasma proteins, negatively charged) are on one side, diffusible ions distribute unequally. The protein side has higher cation and lower diffusible anion concentration. Contributes to slightly higher plasma oncotic pressure. Reference: Guyton — Donnan Equilibrium.

questions.questionText: The indicator used to measure ECF volume is:
questions.explanation: ECF: inulin, mannitol, sucrose (distribute in ECF, don't enter cells). TBW: D2O, tritiated water. Plasma volume: Evans blue (T-1824), 131I-albumin. RBC volume: 51Cr-labeled RBCs. ICF = TBW - ECF (calculated). Reference: Guyton — Compartment Measurement.

questions.questionText: Facilitated diffusion differs from simple diffusion because it:
questions.optionB: Shows saturation kinetics and requires a carrier protein
questions.explanation: Facilitated diffusion: passive, moves DOWN gradient, requires carrier/channel protein → shows saturation kinetics (Vmax), specificity, competitive inhibition. Example: GLUT transporters for glucose. Simple diffusion: no carrier, no saturation. Reference: Guyton — Membrane Transport.

questions.questionText: Gap junctions allow direct cell communication through proteins called:
questions.explanation: Connexins assemble into hexameric connexons. Two connexons from adjacent cells form a complete gap junction channel allowing ions, small molecules (<1000 Da), and second messengers to pass. Important in cardiac muscle (electrical coupling), smooth muscle, and embryonic development. Reference: Guyton — Cell Junctions.

questions.questionText: The Nernst equation is used to calculate the:
questions.optionB: Equilibrium potential for a single ion
questions.explanation: Nernst equation calculates equilibrium potential for a SINGLE ion: E = 61/z × log([ion]o/[ion]i) at 37°C. ENa ≈ +60 mV, EK ≈ -90 mV. Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation calculates RMP using multiple ion permeabilities. Reference: Guyton — Nernst Equation.

questions.questionText: The cAMP second messenger pathway involves activation of:
questions.optionB: Adenylyl cyclase → cAMP → Protein Kinase A
questions.explanation: Ligand → GPCR → Gs → adenylyl cyclase → cAMP → PKA. Examples: ACTH, LH, FSH, TSH, PTH, glucagon, β-adrenergic. Gi inhibits (α2-adrenergic). cAMP degraded by phosphodiesterase. Gq activates PLC → IP3/DAG pathway. Reference: Guyton — Signal Transduction.

questions.questionText: The receptor with intrinsic tyrosine kinase activity is for:
questions.explanation: Insulin receptor is a receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK). Binding causes autophosphorylation → IRS → PI3K/Akt pathway (GLUT4 translocation) and Ras/MAPK (growth). Other RTK ligands: IGF-1, EGF, PDGF, VEGF. Epinephrine, TSH, ACTH use GPCRs. Reference: Guyton — Receptor Types.

questions.questionText: The pH of normal arterial blood is:
questions.explanation: Normal pH = 7.35-7.45 (avg 7.40). Acidemia < 7.35, alkalemia > 7.45. Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = 6.1 + log([HCO3-]/0.03 × PCO2). Normal HCO3- = 24 mEq/L, PCO2 = 40 mmHg. Most important ECF buffer is bicarbonate. Reference: Guyton — Acid-Base Balance.

questions.questionText: The most important extracellular buffer is:
questions.explanation: Bicarbonate is most important ECF buffer: high concentration (24 mEq/L), open system (CO2 regulated by lungs, HCO3- by kidneys). Hemoglobin is most important intracellular/whole blood buffer. Phosphate is important intracellularly and in urine. Reference: Guyton — Buffer Systems.

questions.questionText: The depolarization phase of the neuronal action potential is caused by:
questions.explanation: Voltage-gated Na+ channels open → rapid Na+ influx → membrane potential rises from -70 mV toward +30 mV. Repolarization: Na+ channels inactivate, K+ channels open → K+ efflux. In cardiac muscle, Ca2+ creates plateau phase. Reference: Guyton — Action Potential.

questions.questionText: The absolute refractory period is due to:
questions.optionB: Na+ channels being in the inactivated state
questions.explanation: During ARP, no stimulus can trigger another AP because Na+ channels are INACTIVATED (h-gate closed). They must return to resting state before reopening. During RRP, a stronger stimulus works as some Na+ channels have recovered. Reference: Guyton — Refractory Periods.

questions.questionText: Saltatory conduction occurs in:
questions.explanation: Action potential 'jumps' node to node in myelinated fibers. Myelin (Schwann cells in PNS, oligodendrocytes in CNS) insulates; Na+ channels concentrated at nodes. Much faster (120 m/s Aα fibers) and energy-efficient than continuous conduction in unmyelinated fibers (0.5-2 m/s C fibers). Reference: Guyton — Nerve Conduction.

questions.questionText: The nicotinic acetylcholine receptor is an example of a:
questions.explanation: nAChR is a classic ligand-gated (ionotropic) receptor. ACh binding opens the channel for Na+/K+ → depolarization (EPP at NMJ). Other examples: GABA-A (Cl-), glycine (Cl-), NMDA/AMPA (cations), 5-HT3. Muscarinic receptors are metabotropic (GPCRs). Reference: Guyton — Ion Channels.

questions.questionText: Starling forces governing capillary fluid exchange include all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: 4 Starling forces: (1) Capillary hydrostatic Pc (pushes fluid OUT), (2) Plasma oncotic πp (pulls fluid IN, mainly albumin), (3) Interstitial hydrostatic Pi, (4) Interstitial oncotic πi. Arterial BP is NOT a direct Starling force at the capillary level. Reference: Guyton — Capillary Dynamics.

questions.questionText: The main intracellular cation is:
questions.explanation: K+ is the main ICF cation (~140 mEq/L inside, ~4 mEq/L ECF). Na+ is the main ECF cation (~140 mEq/L). Maintained by Na+/K+ ATPase. Main ICF anion: phosphate. Main ECF anion: Cl-. Reference: Guyton — Ion Distribution.

questions.questionText: Isotonic saline (0.9% NaCl) infusion expands:
questions.explanation: Isotonic saline has same osmolality as plasma → distributes entirely in ECF. 1L: ~250 mL plasma, ~750 mL interstitial (3:1 ratio). 5% dextrose distributes across TBW (ICF+ECF). Hypertonic saline initially expands ECF and draws water from ICF. Reference: Guyton — IV Fluid Physiology.

questions.questionText: The 'all-or-none' law applies to:
questions.explanation: Once threshold is reached, AP fires with FULL amplitude regardless of stimulus strength. Stronger stimuli increase FREQUENCY, not amplitude. Graded potentials vary in amplitude with stimulus strength and decrease with distance (decremental). Reference: Guyton — All-or-None Principle.

questions.questionText: The Gq protein activates which signaling pathway?
questions.explanation: Gq → PLC → PIP2 cleaved into IP3 (releases Ca2+ from ER) and DAG (activates PKC). Examples: α1-adrenergic, M1/M3 muscarinic, H1 histamine, AT1, V1, oxytocin, GnRH, TRH receptors. Gs → adenylyl cyclase. Gi → inhibits adenylyl cyclase. Reference: Guyton — G-Protein Signaling.

questions.questionText: Apoptosis is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Cell shrinkage, chromatin condensation, apoptotic bodies without inflammation
questions.explanation: Apoptosis: orderly cell death with cell shrinkage, chromatin condensation, DNA ladder pattern (180 bp multiples), apoptotic bodies phagocytosed without inflammation. Necrosis: swelling, membrane rupture, inflammation. Mediators: caspases (initiator 8,9; executioner 3,6,7). Bcl-2 is anti-apoptotic. Reference: Guyton — Cell Death.

questions.questionText: SGLT2 in the proximal tubule performs:
questions.optionB: Na+-glucose cotransport (secondary active transport) for glucose reabsorption
questions.explanation: SGLT2 (early PCT) reabsorbs ~90% of filtered glucose via secondary active transport (Na+-glucose cotransport). SGLT1 (late PCT) reabsorbs remaining ~10%. Glucose exits basolaterally via GLUT2. SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) cause therapeutic glucosuria in diabetes. Renal threshold ≈ 180 mg/dL. Reference: Guyton — Renal Glucose.

questions.questionText: The chloride shift (Hamburger phenomenon) occurs in:
questions.optionB: Red blood cells during CO2 transport
questions.explanation: At tissue capillaries: CO2 enters RBCs → carbonic anhydrase → H2CO3 → H+ + HCO3-. HCO3- exits RBC via band 3 (AE1) exchanger in exchange for Cl- entering. Maintains electrical neutrality. Reverse occurs in lungs. ~70% of CO2 carried as HCO3-. Reference: Guyton — CO2 Transport.

questions.questionText: Fick's law states that diffusion rate is directly proportional to:
questions.optionB: Area and concentration gradient, inversely to thickness
questions.explanation: J = -DA(ΔC/Δx). Directly proportional to surface area (A) and concentration gradient (ΔC), inversely to thickness (Δx). Clinically: fibrosis increases thickness (reduces diffusion), emphysema reduces area. Reference: Guyton — Diffusion.

questions.questionText: The cell cycle phase where DNA replication occurs is:
questions.explanation: S phase: DNA synthesis, content doubles (2n→4n). G1: growth, preparation. G2: error checking. M: mitosis + cytokinesis. G0: quiescent (neurons, RBCs). Cyclin-CDK complexes regulate. p53 blocks at G1/S checkpoint if DNA damaged. Reference: Guyton — Cell Division.
